name: Samyak Chakrabarty
role: PhD Student, Electrical and Computer Engineering, UCLA
summary: I am a 3rd Year PhD student under Prof. Sudhakar Pamarti, SP&CE Lab, UCLA, working on Memory design, Compute-in-Memory (CiM) and Hardware accelerators for Machine learning.
about: I am currently pursuing an MS/PhD in Electrical and Computer Engineering at UCLA under Prof. Sudhakar Pamarti. My current research focuses on compute-in-memory systems, memory design, mixed-signal hardware, and accelerator architectures for machine learning.

I received my undergraduate degree in Electronics and Electrical Communication Engineering from IIT Kharagpur, where I built a strong foundation in circuits, systems, signal processing, and VLSI. During my undergraduate years, I worked on diverse hardware-design problems, including charge-trap flash device modeling, oscillator and clocking circuits, and FPGA-based accelerator design, with research experiences at IIT Bombay and the University of British Columbia.

My broader interest lies in building efficient intelligent hardware by connecting innovations across devices, circuits, and architectures, and turning them into practical systems that deliver gains in density, speed, and energy efficiency.

researchAreas.title: Attention Accelerator for ViT Using DRAM-CIM Architecture
researchAreas.description: Master's thesis project at UCLA on DRAM-CIM acceleration for vision transformers.
bullets: Proposed a DRAM-based bit-cell architecture that improves compute density, accuracy, and energy efficiency for ViT attention.
Designed circuit schematics and layouts, and ran Cadence Virtuoso simulations for power, area, and linearity across process corners.
Co-developed a system-level integration flow to evaluate accuracy, throughput, power, and hardware-software tradeoffs.

researchAreas.title: CiM Hardware Accelerator for ResNet-18 in GF22FDSOI
researchAreas.description: DARPA-sponsored compute-in-memory accelerator project in GlobalFoundries 22nm.
bullets: Developed RTL architecture and digital plus mixed-signal verification for interfacing with an analog compute-in-memory macro.
Designed a compact memory layout achieving 18 Mb/mm2 weight density and proposed system-level optimizations for accuracy and throughput.
Automated the RTL-to-GDSII flow, integrated multiple IPs, and achieved timing closure at 1 GHz for successful silicon tapeout.

researchAreas.title: A Light FPGA-based Accelerator for ANN Training
researchAreas.description: Bachelor's thesis on low-energy FPGA acceleration for neural-network training.
bullets: Designed a low-energy hardware accelerator on FPGA in SystemVerilog and evaluated timing and power using Vivado.
Used SIMD, instruction pipelining, and parallelism to build a full training-oriented processor architecture.
Achieved about 60% area reduction and 2x speedup while maintaining 95.18% accuracy on MNIST.

researchAreas.title: Intuitive Design and Analysis Method for MPROs
researchAreas.description: Summer internship project on multipath ring oscillators and oscillator classification.
bullets: Simulated multipath ring oscillators in Cadence Virtuoso and developed a classification method based on ring and wiring modes.
Performed time-domain, frequency-domain, phase-noise, and mismatch analyses in Cadence.
Validated the methodology using post-layout simulations and silicon measurements on a TSMC 65nm test chip.

researchAreas.title: Vt-Degradation Effect of Charge Trap Flash and Impact on ANN Training
researchAreas.description: Internship project on charge-trap flash modeling for analog neural-network training.
bullets: Developed a MATLAB model of charge-trap flash for resistive processing units based on experimental data under stochastic pulse trains.
Analyzed the impact of Vt degradation and other non-idealities on ANN training to determine suitable pulse width and height.
Built a SPICE model of the CTF transistor and its program/erase behavior using dependent sources and capacitors.

publications.title: MXFormer: A Microscaling Floating Point Charge-Trap Transistor Compute-in-Memory Transformer Accelerator
publications.authors: G. Karfakis, S. Chakrabarty, V. Jacob, S. Qiao, S. Iyer, S. Pamarti, P. Gupta
publications.abstract: MXFormer is a hybrid, weight-stationary transformer accelerator that uses ultra-dense charge-trap-transistor compute-in-memory arrays with MXFP4 microscaling floating point to keep hundreds of millions of parameters on chip. By combining analog CTT arrays for static-weight layers with digital units for attention and normalization, it improves compute density and energy efficiency while preserving near-digital accuracy without retraining.

publications.title: Back-gate Mediated VTH Mismatch Reduction to Enable 6-bit Precision in FD-SOI Charge-Trap Transistor
publications.authors: S. Qiao, S. Wang, J. Zhu, Z. Guo, M. Zeinali, V. Jacob, S. Chakrabarty, B. Vaisband, S. Iyer, S. Pamarti
publications.abstract: This paper presents a method to reduce intrinsic VTH mismatch in charge-trap transistors by inducing charge trapping near the interface between the ultra-thin silicon channel and the buried oxide in FD-SOI technology, independent of conventional trapping in the high-k gate dielectric. The technique reduces VTH variation by more than 3x, enables 5-bit programmability in the subthreshold region for area-efficient analog compute-in-memory, and can be extended to mismatch reduction in circuits such as sense amplifiers and SRAMs.
